11: soter hos estin Christos Kyrios — a Savior who is Christ the Lord — the angelic birth announcement stacks three titles on the newborn: soter (savior — the most frequent soteriological title in Luke-Acts, used 4x in Luke, rarely in Matthew/Mark), Christos (the Messiah), and Kyrios (Lord — the LXX name for YHWH). The combination of all three titles in one announcement is Luke's programmatic Christological declaration. en polei David — in the city of David — the Davidic geography grounds the birth in covenant geography.
29: nyn apolueis ton doulon sou Despota — now you are releasing your servant, Master, in peace — the Nunc Dimittis of Simeon is structured as a release-prayer: apolueis (you release, let go) uses the legal-release term for a slave being freed or a prisoner being discharged; Simeon describes his death as a release from watchful-keeping-duty, now that he has seen the salvation. Despota (Master, lord with absolute authority) is the prayer address; eirene (peace) is the shalom of complete wellbeing at death's threshold.
1: apographesthai pasan ten oikoumenen — to register all the inhabited world — apographē (enrollment, registration) is an administrative census for taxation; oikoumenē (the inhabited world) is Luke's preferred term for the Roman empire. The dating reference to Quirinius (hēgemoneuontos) is the most discussed chronological problem in Luke — Josephus places the Quirinius census in 6-7 CE, not at Jesus's birth. Various solutions include a prior legateship or an earlier enrollment; the historical tension does not affect the theological point Luke is making: the Roman empire's administrative machinery becomes the instrument of the Davidic birthplace prophecy.
2: hautē apographē prōtē egeneto — this registration was the first — the phrase prōtē egeneto may be translated "first took place" (superlative) or "was before" (comparative), which some read as "this registration was before the one Quirinius made [as governor]." The syntactical ambiguity has been the source of extensive debate. Luke's purpose is the historical anchoring characteristic of his prologue method (1:1-4): he is demonstrating that the birth of Christ occurred within datable human history.
3: eporeuonto pantes apographesthai — all went to be registered — the imperfect eporeuonto (were going, were traveling) depicts the ongoing movement of population. The mass registration is the historical mechanism through which the Davidic birthplace requirement is met; Luke presents the Roman political order as an unwitting instrument of divine providence without labeling it as such.
4: anebē de kai Iōsēph apo tēs Galilaias ek poleōs Nazareth — Joseph also went up from Galilee, from Nazareth — anebainō (go up) is the standard verb for travel to Jerusalem/Judea, as higher elevation; the journey is the fulfillment of the Davidic-birthplace requirement. Polin Dauid (city of David) is Bethlehem, not Jerusalem — Luke is precise about which city of David is intended.
5: engkuō ousē — who was pregnant — engkuos (pregnant, great with child) is a medical term, consistent with Luke's medical vocabulary (noted by Hobart). The reference to Mary as Joseph's memnesteumenē (promised in marriage, betrothed) at this point of pregnancy maintains the earlier annunciation narrative's implication without requiring an explanation Luke has already provided.
6: eplēsthēsan hai hēmerai tou tekein autēn — the days for her to give birth were fulfilled — eplēsthēsan (were fulfilled, were completed) is Luke's fulfillment language; the same verb appears throughout Luke-Acts when divine plans reach completion. Tekein (to give birth, infinitive of tiktō) is the verb of the annunciation (1:31); the verb link connects the promise to the fulfillment.
7: ton huion autēs ton prōtotokon — her firstborn son — prōtotokos (firstborn) has both legal and theological weight: the firstborn son receives the covenant inheritance and consecration to God (Exod 13:2; Num 18:15-16). The term does not say anything about whether Mary had later children — that is determined by other texts. espargānōsen — she wrapped him in swaddling cloths — sparganoa are strips of cloth for swaddling infants (a standard Greek medical term); Luke's use of the term is both precise and symbolic. phatnē — manger — the manger is the sign in v.12; the word means an animal feeding trough, not necessarily a stable (kataluma = lodging place, not necessarily an inn).
8: poimenes — shepherds — the choice of shepherds as the first recipients of the angelic announcement is significant against the background that shepherds in Second Temple Palestine were often considered ritually unreliable (unable to testify in court, frequently in contact with what was considered unclean). Luke presents the announcement going first to those society marginalized. agraulountes — living in the fields — compound of agros (field) + aulē (courtyard, dwelling); they sleep in the open field rather than in shelter.
9: angelos kyriou epestē autois — an angel of the Lord appeared to them — ephistēmi (stand upon, appear suddenly) is Luke's preferred verb for angelic appearance (cf. 1:11; Acts 12:7; 23:11); it conveys sudden presence rather than gradual approach. doxa kyriou — the glory of the Lord — doxa here is the Shekinah-light of divine presence; the same luminous manifestation that marked the tabernacle and temple. The shepherds' fear is the standard response to holy divine presence.
10: euangelizomai hymin charan megalēn — I bring you good news of great joy — euangelizomai is Luke's distinctive verb (used 10 times in Luke, 15 in Acts, rare elsewhere in the Gospels); it is the proclamation-verb of Isa 61:1 (the Servant announcing good news). The dative hymin (to you — the shepherds, present) followed by panti tō laō (for all the people, future/broader) traces the scope: the announcement begins with the marginalized few and extends to all Israel.
12: kai touto hymin to sēmeion — and this will be the sign for you — the sēmeion (sign) is striking: not a miraculous portent but a baby in a manger. The sign is the incarnation itself — the lowliness of the setting confirms, rather than contradicts, that this is the messianic Savior. The phatin (manger) as sign echoes the Isaianic servant-sign of the child: the expected messianic triumph comes in unexpected lowliness.
13: stratias ouraniou — a heavenly army/host — stratia is the military term (army, host); the genitive ouraniou (heavenly) qualifies the army's origin. The heavenly host both worships (as the angels of God) and stands ready for battle — the dual meaning of the divine army. The praise they offer (v.14) transforms the military assembly into a liturgical choir, though the military connotation remains: the hosts of heaven declare the peace that Christ's coming brings.
14: en anthrōpois eudokias — among people of his good pleasure/favor — the genitive eudokias (of good will, of favor) is the crucial textual and theological crux. The earliest and best manuscripts (p75, B, Sinaiticus, A) read eudokias (genitive), making the phrase "peace to people of his good will/favor" — those whom God favors, the recipients of his gracious election. A later reading, eudokia (nominative), yields "peace on earth, good will to men" — making good will the third coordinate gift. The genitive is almost certainly original (lectio difficilior; reflected in Jerome's "bonae voluntatis") and is theologically richer: the peace announced is not universal but directed to those within the sphere of divine grace. The Qumran texts similarly use "men of his good pleasure" (bnei rezono) as a technical term for the covenant community.
15: hōs apēlthon ap autōn — when they had gone from them — the aorist genitive absolute apelthontōn (having departed) is a clean narrative transition. The shepherds' immediate response dielthoumen (let us go through — first person plural aorist subjunctive hortatory) signals communal decision and action. Rēma (word, thing) at the end — "this thing that has happened" — uses the theologically loaded word for divine speech/deed throughout Luke-Acts.
16: aneuron te tēn Mariam — they found Mary — aneuriskō (find by searching) is a compound form of heuriskō that emphasizes the searching process; they located what they sought by inquiry. Spoudē (haste) in v.16 describes their movement — not reckless urgency but eager intentionality. The discovery confirms the sign: the baby in the manger is exactly as the angel said.
17: egnōrisan peri tou rēmatos — they made known the word — gnōrizō (make known, reveal) is used for the public proclamation of what was privately received; the shepherds become the first evangelists, announcing to others what the angel announced to them. The pattern — receive, go, make known — is the basic missionary structure that Luke will develop throughout Acts.
18: pantes hoi akousantes — all who heard — Luke emphasizes the crowd's amazed response (ethaumasan — they were amazed, marveled) as the standard human response to the divine breaking into ordinary life. The imperfect ethaumasan (were marveling) suggests ongoing astonishment, not a momentary reaction. The amazement does not necessarily indicate faith; it is the raw material from which faith can grow.
19: hē de Mariam panta sunētērei ta rēmata tauta sunballousa en tē kardia autēs — Mary kept all these things and pondered them in her heart — two participles describe Mary's interior response. Suntēreō (keep together, treasure up) suggests active preservation of memory; it appears in Dan 7:28 LXX for Daniel preserving the vision in his heart. Sunballousa (pondering, throwing together) from sunballō (to throw together, compare) suggests the active work of interpretation — Mary is comparing, correlating, and seeking to understand the meaning of what is happening. This verse establishes Mary as the contemplative witness whose perspective shapes Luke's infancy narrative.
20: hupestrepsan hoi poimenes — the shepherds returned — hupostrephō (return) is one of Luke's most frequent verbs (32 times in Luke-Acts vs. 3 in Matthew-Mark-John). The return with praise and glory to God is the standard Lukan conclusion to a healing/salvation event: the recipients of grace return to their lives glorifying God. Doxazontes kai ainountes (glorifying and praising) are the paired verbs of worship throughout Luke-Acts.
21: hote eplēsthēsan hēmerai oktō tou peritemein auton — when eight days were fulfilled for circumcising him — eplēsthēsan (were fulfilled) is Luke's covenant-completion language; the eighth day circumcision is the Abrahamic covenant sign (Gen 17:12; Lev 12:3). Peritemein (to circumcise, infinitive of peritemnō) places Jesus within the covenant community from infancy. The name Iēsous is given at circumcision as the angel commanded (1:31) — the name and covenant sign are received together.
22: hote eplēsthēsan hai hēmerai tou katharismou autōn kata ton nomon Mōuseōs — when the days of their purification according to the law of Moses were completed — the katharismos (purification) follows Lev 12:1-8: 40 days after the birth of a son, the mother undergoes a purification rite at the temple. The pronoun autōn (their) is unusual — purification was the mother's rite, not both parents'; some suggest Luke is assimilating the purification to a presentation. Anagagein (to bring up, present) is the presentation vocabulary.
23: kathōs gegraptai en nomō kyriou — as it is written in the law of the Lord — the citation follows Exod 13:2, 12 (every firstborn male is holy to the Lord). Luke uses prōtotokon (firstborn) to connect this verse to v.7; the consecration of the firstborn in the law is the legal context for Jesus's presentation. Hagion tō kyriō klēthēsetai (he will be called holy to the Lord) echoes the annunciation language (1:35, hagion klēthēsetai).
24: zeugos trugonōn ē duo nossous peristerōn — a pair of turtledoves or two young pigeons — the offering is the poor woman's alternative prescribed in Lev 12:8 (when she cannot afford a lamb). Luke's inclusion of this detail establishes the family's economic status — they are among the anawim, the humble poor of Israel — and foreshadows the Magnificat's theme (the humble lifted up). The use of zeugos (yoke, pair) for the birds is precise.
25: paraklēsin tou Israēl — the consolation of Israel — paraklēsis is one of Luke's theologically loaded terms: consolation, comfort, encouragement. It echoes Isa 40:1 (the opening of Deutero-Isaiah: "Comfort, comfort my people") — the messianic expectation that God would return to comfort his exiled, suffering people. Simeon's hope is the hope of Isa 40-66 in a single word. pneuma hagion ēn ep auton — the Holy Spirit was upon him — the threefold Spirit-reference in vv.25-27 (upon him, revealed to him, moved by him) structures the Nunc Dimittis scene as a pneumatic event.
26: kechrēmatismenon hypo tou pneumatos tou hagiou — divinely revealed/warned by the Holy Spirit — chrēmatizō is the technical term for receiving a divine oracle or divine instruction (used for the Magi's warning in Matt 2:12; for Noah in Heb 11:7); it conveys authoritative divine communication. The content of the revelation — mē idein thanaton prin an idē ton Christon kyriou (he would not see death before seeing the Lord's Christ) — ties Simeon's hope to the specific messianic moment.
27: en tō pneumati — in/by the Spirit — the preposition en with pneumati can be instrumental (by the Spirit) or locative (in the sphere of the Spirit); the ambiguity is typically Lukan. Simeon's Spirit-directed movement to the temple and the parents' bringing of Jesus at the same moment is presented as providential coordination — both parties are following separate promptings that converge in the temple.
28: edexato auto eis tas agklas autou — he took him up in his arms — agkale (arm, elbow) is the word for the bent arm that cradles a child; the physical gesture of reception is the enacted reception of the Messiah by old Israel. Simeon holding the infant Jesus is the visual image that the Nunc Dimittis makes verbal: he has received the salvation his nation waited for.
30: hoti eidon hoi ophthalmoi mou to sōtērion sou — for my eyes have seen your salvation — sōtērion (salvation as a noun/neuter) is Luke's preferred form (also 3:6; Acts 28:28); the phrase echoes Ps 98:2-3 LXX (eidon panta ta perata tēs gēs to sōtērion tou theou hēmōn). The salvation that is seen by the eyes is the infant — salvation is personified in Christ, not merely a concept.
31: ho hetoimasas kata prosōpon pantōn tōn laōn — which you have prepared in the presence of all peoples — hetoimazō (prepare) echoes Mal 3:1 and the Baptist's preparation language; YHWH has prepared the salvation in advance. Kata prosōpon pantōn tōn laōn (in the face/presence of all peoples) — the salvation is public, not private or esoteric.
32: phōs eis apokalupsin ethnōn — a light for revelation to the Gentiles — apokalupsis (unveiling, revelation) combined with phōs (light) draws on Isa 42:6 and 49:6 (the Servant as light to the nations). Ethnōn (Gentiles/nations) is the first explicit mention of Gentile inclusion in Luke's birth narrative — the scope of salvation transcends Israel. doxan laou sou Israēl — the glory of your people Israel — the salvation is for Israel's glory as well; the Gentile inclusion does not displace Israel's priority but extends the benefit.
33: kai ēn ho patēr autou kai hē mētēr thaumazontes — his father and mother were amazed — Luke uses ho patēr autou (his father) for Joseph without theological embarrassment, consistent with the legal/social reality that Joseph is Jesus's father in every sense except biological generation. The amazement (thaumazō) at Simeon's words about Jesus is the same response as the shepherds' audience (v.18) — the appropriate human response to the unexpected scope of the divine plan.
34: keimai eis ptōsin kai anastasin pollōn en tō Israēl — set for the fall and rising of many in Israel — ptōsis (fall) and anastasis (rising) are the two responses Jesus will produce in Israel; the same Greek word anastasis is used for both the resurrection and the "rising" here. sēmeion antilegomenon — a sign that is spoken against/contradicted — antilegō (speak against, contradict) anticipates the opposition that will define Jesus's ministry and lead to the cross. The sign that was the silent baby in the manger will become the controversial rabbi and the executed Messiah.
35: rhomphaia — a sword — the rhomphaia is the large, curved Thracian sword (as opposed to the shorter machaira); used in LXX for the sword that drove Adam from Eden (Gen 3:24), and the sword of divine judgment throughout the prophets. The sword that will pierce Mary's soul is the sword of grief at the cross — but also, perhaps, the sword of the word itself (Heb 4:12). hopōs an apokalupphthōsin ek pollōn kardiōn dialogismoi — so that thoughts from many hearts will be revealed — dialogismos (reasoning, internal debate) is consistently negative in Luke; the revealing of hearts is the effect of encountering Christ.
36: prophetis Hanna — a prophetess, Anna — prophētis (prophetess, feminine form) is the technical term for one who speaks under divine inspiration; Anna is one of the few NT women explicitly named prophets. The genealogical specificity (thugatēr Phanouel ek phylēs Asēr — daughter of Phanuel, of the tribe of Asher) is unusual for a woman, suggesting her public significance. The note about her age is textually complex: she had lived with her husband seven years, then was a widow of/for 84 years — placing her age at approximately 105 if widowed at 14, or the 84 years may describe the period of her widowhood as the total.
37: nēsteiais kai deēsesin latreuousa nykta kai hēmeran — worshiping with fasting and prayer night and day — latreuō (worship, serve with religious devotion) is the liturgical service term; Anna's fasting and prayer is continuous, structured worship, not occasional piety. The night-and-day formula (nykta kai hēmeran) emphasizes the comprehensive, uninterrupted nature of her devotion — the posture of one wholly consecrated to anticipating the messianic redemption.
38: lytrōsin Ierousalēm — the redemption of Jerusalem — lytrōsis (redemption, ransom-release) echoes lytron (ransom) and the LXX release terminology; it is the technical term for the covenant redemption of God's people (Ps 111:9 LXX). The phrase "redemption of Jerusalem" situates Anna's hope in the nationalprophetic expectation (Isa 52:9 — "for the LORD has redeemed Jerusalem") that the city's exile condition would end with the Messiah's coming.
39: hōs etelesan panta ta kata ton nomon kyriou — when they had performed all things according to the law of the Lord — Luke's telos-language (teleoō = complete, fulfill) here describes legal compliance rather than prophetic fulfillment, but the vocabulary overlap is deliberate. The family's complete Torah observance at the birth establishes Jesus's continuity with the covenant community even as his identity transcends it.
40: to de paidion ēuxanen kai ekrataiouto plēroumenon sophia — the child grew and became strong, filled with wisdom — auxanō (grow) and krataioomai (become strong) are Luke's standard developmental verbs (also 1:80 for John); both are imperfects, indicating gradual progress over time. plēroumenon sophia — filled with wisdom — plēroō (fill, fulfill) with sophia (wisdom) describes the progressive increase of wisdom in the growing child; the divine wisdom fills him as he grows into his human capacities. Charis theou ēn ep auton — the grace of God was upon him — the same formula as Elizabeth's womb-grace for John (1:66); divine favor rests on the child continuously.
41: kai eporeuonto hoi goneis autou kat etos eis Ierousalem — his parents went to Jerusalem every year — kata etos (year by year, annually) describes the established practice of Passover pilgrimage. The Deuteronomic law required three annual festivals (Deut 16:16) but Passover was the primary requirement observed by devout families. Luke's specification of the parents' annual practice establishes the piety context for the temple episode and shows that Jesus grew up within regular Passover observance.
42: hote egeneto etōn dōdeka — when he was twelve years old — the age of 12 is significant in Jewish practice as approaching bar mitzvah responsibility (technically 13 in rabbinic tradition, but the approach to legal adulthood began earlier). Luke specifies this age because it marks the threshold of religious majority at which a boy begins to take personal responsibility for Torah observance. This is Jesus's first appearance as a speaking actor in the narrative.
43: hupestreyen ho pais Iēsous en Ierousalēm — the boy Jesus remained in Jerusalem — hypomenō (remain behind, stay) rather than hupostrephō (return) signals that Jesus did not return with the caravan; he stayed deliberately. Hoi goneis autou ouk egnōsan (his parents did not know) — the unknown is their not knowing that he stayed, not a mistake on Jesus's part. They assumed he was in the synōdia (traveling company, caravan) — the large group of Galilean pilgrims traveling together.
44: nomisantes de auton einai en tē synōdia — supposing him to be in the group — synōdia (company, caravan, traveling group) is a hapax in the NT; the compound (syn = with + hodos = road, journey) describes the pilgrimage group. The assumption that Jesus was somewhere in the group is reasonable — in large caravan travel, extended family and acquaintances would spread across the group. A day's journey would have carried them well away from Jerusalem before the absence was noticed.
45: anastraphentes de anezētoun auton — when they did not find him, they returned, searching — anazēteō (search for, seek up and down) is an intensive compound of zēteō (seek); the ana- prefix suggests thorough searching in every direction. The three days of searching (one day traveling, one returning, one day in Jerusalem) before finding him in the temple is Luke's only narrative that places Jesus in a human learning environment.
46: en tō hierō kathezomenon en mesō tōn didaskalōn — sitting in the temple among the teachers — didaskālos (teacher, doctor of the law) in the plural indicates the professional scribal teachers who taught in the temple courts. Kathezomenon en mesō (sitting in the middle) is the posture of a student participating in instruction — sitting on the floor among the teachers (who sat on chairs or benches, the elevated teaching position). The teachers are asking questions and he is answering; but the direction of inquiry suggests that both parties are engaged in the Socratic dialogue of Jewish learning.
47: existanto de pantes hoi akouontes autou epi tē sunēsei kai tais apokrisesin autou — all who heard him were astonished at his understanding and his answers — existēmi (stand outside, be astonished — from which we get ekstasis) describes the teachers' reaction to his answers. Sunesis (understanding, comprehension) is the cognitive intelligence; apokrisis (answer, response) is the verbal expression. The astonishment of the professional teachers is not patronizing (smart child!) but genuine recognition of exceptional insight.
48: odunōmenoi — anguished — the word is one of Luke's strongest emotional terms (used for the rich man's anguish in Hades at 16:24-25; for Paul's associates's grief at Acts 20:38). Mary's report uses the parental first-person dual — ho patēr sou kagō (your father and I) — with Joseph named as father; Luke does not soften the parental perspective to exclude Joseph.
49: dei me einai en tois tou patros mou — I must be in my Father's things/house — the crucial phrase is tois tou patros mou (the things/house of my Father). The Greek phrase is deliberately ambiguous: ta tou patros can mean "my Father's house" (oikos being understood), "my Father's affairs/business," or "the things that belong to my Father." Early translations diverged: the Vulgate has in his quae Patris mei sunt (in the things that are my Father's); English versions split between "house" and "business/affairs." The ambiguity is productive — in the temple he is in his Father's house AND doing his Father's business. dei — must, it is necessary — the divine necessity (dei) that governs Jesus's mission throughout Luke (4:43; 9:22; 13:33; 17:25; 24:7) appears here at its first speaking: his entire life is shaped by this necessity.
50: kai autoi ou sunēkan to rēma — and they did not understand the word — their failure to understand (ou sunēkan, from suniēmi — to understand, put together) establishes a pattern that runs throughout Luke: those closest to Jesus repeatedly fail to grasp the meaning of his words, especially those concerning the dei of his mission. Understanding comes only after the resurrection (24:45).
51: kai katebē met autōn kai ēlthen eis Nazareth kai ēn hypotassomenos autois — he went down with them and came to Nazareth and was subject/submitting to them — hypotassō (subject, subordinate, arrange under) in the present participle (hypotassomenos) describes an ongoing, voluntary self-subordination of the twelve-year-old who has just spoken of divine necessity. The one who must be in his Father's affairs voluntarily places himself under parental authority — the same paradox of the incarnation that Phil 2:6-8 articulates theologically. Hē de mētēr autou dietērei panta ta rēmata tauta en tē kardia autēs — his mother treasured all these things in her heart — the second Mary-treasuring notice (cf. 2:19), bookending the temple episode.
52: Iēsous proekopten en tē sophia kai hēlikia kai chariti para theō kai anthrōpois — Jesus advanced in wisdom and stature and favor with God and men — prokoptō (advance, make progress, cut a path forward) is an imperfect indicating gradual development over time. The fourfold description — wisdom (sophia), age/stature (hēlikia), grace with God (charis para theō), grace with people (charis para anthrōpois) — is modeled on 1 Sam 2:26 (Samuel grew in favor with both God and men). Luke presents Jesus's development as a genuine human growth process, consistent with the incarnation; the one who had divine wisdom grew in its human expression.
